coredata.dc:title: High-performance asymmetric supercapacitor based on flowery nickel-zinc phosphate microspheres with carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Manying
Wang, Shaohua
Zhao, Lijun
Guo, Zuoxing
coredata.dc:description: Despite of high theoretical capacity, transition metal phosphates always suffer from obvious volume expansion and poor conductivity in electrochemical reaction, thus resulting in rapid capacity fade and restricted comprehensive performance. In this work, we used a simple hydrothermal route to graft carbon dots on the binary mulberry-like Ni-Zn phosphates (CDs/NZP). Introducing carbon dots could not only raise the 50.9% specific capacitance at 1 A g−1 than bare Ni-Zn phosphates, but also enhance long-term cycling duration. The enhanced capacitance performance of CDs/NZP greatly contribute to synergistic effect, wherein mulberry-like CDs/NZP-10 structure achieve high-performance energy conversion, while CDs could provide a mechanical protector for mulberry-like structure and simulate fast charge transport. Furthermore, an asymmetric supercapacitor was prepared, utilizing positive CDs/NZP-10 and negative activated carbon (AC), respectively. The asymmetric device displays a high specific capacitance of 89.2 F g−1 (at 1 A g−1) and suitable energy density of 33.7 Wh kg−1 (at 824.9 W kg−1). The low cost, easy preparation and suitable electrochemical performance endow CDs/NZP-10 with voltage application in supercapacitor industry.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Ni-Zn phosphate
Synergistic effect

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD pattern of nickel-zinc composite; high resolution XPS of (b) Ni 2p; (c) Zn 2p; (d) P 2p; (e) C 1s and (f) O 1s.
(a) Raman spectrum of CDs/NZP-10; (b) Raman spectrum of calcined CDs/NZP-10 and carbon dots.
(a, b) TEM image of different magnifications of CDs/NZP-10; inset of Fig. 3c shows the HRTEM of carbon dots; (c) elemental mapping of CDs/NZP-10; (d) EDS spectrometry.

(a) CV curves of CDs/NP-10, CDs/NZP-0, CDs/NZP-5, CDs/NZP-10 and CDs/NZP-20 electrodes at a scanning rate of 10 mV s−1; (b) CV curves of the CDs/NZP-10 with various scanning rates and the inset shows the corresponding current density (i) Ia/Ic-v1/2 (scan rate1/2) plots; (c) GCD curves of CDs/NZP-10 from 1 to 10 A g−1; (d) the specific capacitance of CDs/NP-10, CDs/NZP-0, CDs/NZP-5, CDs/NZP-10 and CDs/NZP-20; (e) the cyclic stability of CDs/NZP-0, CDs/NZP-5 and CDs/NZP-10; (f) the EIS diagram of CDs/NZP-0 and CDs/NZP-10.

(a) CV curves of CDs/NZP-10//AC at scanning rates from 5 mV s−1 to 100 mV s−1; (b) CV curves of CDs/NZP-0//AC at scanning rates from 10 mV s−1 to 100 mV s−1; (c) GCD curves of CDs/NZP-10//AC from 1 A g−1 to 10 A g−1; (d) GCD curves of CDs/NZP-0//AC from 1 A g−1 to 10 A g−1; (e) specific capacitance values and (f) Ragone plot of CDs/NZP-10//AC and CDs/NZP-0//AC; (g) schematic of the asymmetric supercapacitor and (h) cycle performance of CDs/NZP-10//AC at 2 A g−1, and LED lighting image (inset).
Schematic illustration of the morphology transformation at different concentration of adding carbon dots and the path of electronic transmission.
Comparison of electrode specific capacitance and ASC energy density, power density and capacity retention with related works.

head.ce:title: High-performance asymmetric supercapacitor based on flowery nickel-zinc phosphate microspheres with carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Automobile Materials, Ministry of Education and School of Materials Science and Engineering, Jilin University, Changchun, 130022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Ministry of Education and School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Despite of high theoretical capacity, transition metal phosphates always suffer from obvious volume expansion and poor conductivity in electrochemical reaction, thus resulting in rapid capacity fade and restricted comprehensive performance. In this work, we used a simple hydrothermal route to graft carbon dots on the binary mulberry-like Ni-Zn phosphates (CDs/NZP). Introducing carbon dots could not only raise the 50.9% specific capacitance at 1 A g−1 than bare Ni-Zn phosphates, but also enhance long-term cycling duration. The enhanced capacitance performance of CDs/NZP greatly contribute to synergistic effect, wherein mulberry-like CDs/NZP-10 structure achieve high-performance energy conversion, while CDs could provide a mechanical protector for mulberry-like structure and simulate fast charge transport. Furthermore, an asymmetric supercapacitor was prepared, utilizing positive CDs/NZP-10 and negative activated carbon (AC), respectively. The asymmetric device displays a high specific capacitance of 89.2 F g−1 (at 1 A g−1) and suitable energy density of 33.7 Wh kg−1 (at 824.9 W kg−1). The low cost, easy preparation and suitable electrochemical performance endow CDs/NZP-10 with voltage application in supercapacitor industry.

Mulberry-like nickel-zinc phosphate composite decorated with carbon dots was synthesized and employed for high-performance supercapacitor electrode.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, supercapacitors (SCs) have drawn a great deal of attentions due to higher power density and greater cycling lifespan than lithium-ion batteries [1,2]. However, low energy density and high output cost of SCs have been identified as the main bottlenecks, which are detrimental to for the extensive application of SCs [3–5]. Many high-capacitance electrodes have been explored in order to improve the energy density of SCs [6,7]. In the SC electrode materials, the pseudocapacitve materials display higher capacitance performance than electric double layer material, due to the occurrence of fast Faradic redox reaction. Even so, the actual capacitance of SCs remains low values than theoretical capacitance especially at high scanning rates, which is affected by lower electrochemical activity [8–10]. Thus, how to enhance the electrochemical activity of pseudocapacitve materials is crucial to efficiently promote the comprehensive performance of SC devices.

Transition metal phosphates (TMPs) are regarded as one attractive pseudocapacitive materials, owing to high theoretical capacitance and low synthetic temperature [11,12]. To date, various TMPs including Ni2P2O7, VOPO4 and Co3(PO4)2, have been synthesized and used as electrode materials for SCs [13–15]. However, most TMP electrodes always suffer from poor durability and limited rate capability, which has a close relationship with irreversible phase transformation, obvious volume expansion, and low conductivity [16–18]. Many works reported that bimetal TMPs always displays enhanced electrochemical performance than monometal TMPs, because of more active sites and enhanced conductivity [19,20]. For example, a mesoporous NixCo3-x(PO4)2 hollow material synthesized by Zhang et al. has a specific capacitance of 940 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1 and maintains 85% retention rate after 1000 cycles [21].

As well know, the electrochemical reaction of pseudocapacitive materials mainly occurred at the surface or near-surface area [22]. Flowery nanosphere structure will displays open space and exposure more surface area, which helps to fast contact between electrolyte and electrode materials. Moreover, a low conductivity of electrode material will greatly restrict the charge transfer, thus weakening the capacitance performance. Recently, some common expensive carbon materials such as carbon nanosphere [23], graphene oxide [24] were utilized to wrap the electrode materials, which could mechanically reinforce the electrode materials and form conductive path. Even though this hybrid structure could significantly improve the conductivity and stability of active materials, the surface contact between electrolyte and active materials could be retained [17]. By contrast, carbon dots (CDs) display versatile advantages in the low cost, high conductivity and ultrafine size, which could play an important role in protecting structure and promoting conductivity not at the cost of surface wettability [25,26]. For efficiently address these issues, the key lies in rational design of electrode materials. To the best of our knowledge, there is very few work to study the electrochemical performance of nickel-zinc phosphates hybrids combined with carbon dots using such simple route but with high-performance.

Herein, we synthesized Ni-Zn phosphates combined with carbon dots by a simple hydrothermal reaction. By adjusting the reaction concentration of the CDs, the performance of hybrid structure could be greatly enhanced. The synergistic effect of bimetal phosphates and introduction of CDs could efficiently improve the structure stability and conductivity, thus accelerating charge transfer even at high current densities. Served as pseudocapacitive electrode, the nanohybrid presents high specific capacitance of 1885.7 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 and 98.7% retention even after 3000 cycles, which mainly results from the mulberry-like structure, synergistic effect of bimetal phosphates and ultrafine CDs with high conductivity. The asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) based on this hybrid material, which also displays excellent capacitance performance specific capacitance (specific capacitance of 89.2 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 and energy density of 33.7 Wh kg−1 at 824.9 W kg−1).

Carbon dots were synthesized according to the literature [25]. 0.1 g of citric acid, 300 μL of ethylenediamine (EDA) and 0.2 g of P2O5 were added to the Teflon liner followed by the addition of 500 μL of deionized (DI) water. The sealed autoclave was heated at 110 °C for 4 h. After cooling to a room temperature, 5 mL of water was added to the reaction to form a clear solution. The CDs were obtained by adding ethanol to the solution and collecting the precipitate after centrifugation. The precipitates were washed with ethanol and further dried in a vacuum oven at 40 °C for 24 h.

The 10 mg of as-prepared CDs was dispersed in 20 mL of DI water/anhydrous ethanol mixture solution (V1:V2 = 1:1). The well-designed strategy by adjusting the mass of CDs to analyze the effect of carbon dots concentration on morphology and electrochemical performance is shown in Scheme 1 . First, 1.6 mmol of Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, 0.4 mmol of Zn(NO3)2·6H2O and 0.5 g of Na4P2O7 were added to the 20 mL of DI water/anhydrous ethanol mixture solution (V1:V2 = 1:1) and ultrasonicated for 10 min. Then the as-prepared CDs with different concentration range from 0 to 20 mg were dispersed in appeal solution, respectively and stirred for 20 min. The solution was then transferred to a 50 mL Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave, which was kept at 160 °C for 8 h. After cooling to room temperature, the product was washed several times with DI water and ethanol. The as-obtained sample was marked as CDs/NZP-10.

Similarly, we tuned the concentrate of CDs. We change the CDs concentrate from 0 mg, 5 mg–20 mg in turn, and other conditions keep changeless. Then, the as-obtained Ni-Zn phosphate samples were named as CDs/NZP-0, CDs/NZP-5, and CDs/NZP-20, respectively.

Furthermore, we adjusted the ratio of Ni(NO3)2·6H2O and Zn(NO3)2·6H2O compared with preparation method of CDs/NZP-10. We added pure metal nitrate (2 mmol Ni(NO3)2·6H2O or 2 mmol Zn(NO3)2·6H2O), and other conditions keep changeless. Then, the as-obtained phosphate samples were named as CDs/NP-10 and CDs/ZP-10., respectively.

The synthesized product was characterized by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku D/Max 2500, Cu Kα radiation: λ = 1.54156 Å) to identify the crystalline amorphous state and the qualitative analysis. Field emission electron microscopy (FESEM, JEOL JSM-6700F), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL 2100F), energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) were used to measure the morphology and the particle size of the samples. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed with Al Kα rays on an ESCAlab 220i-XL electronic spectrometer manufactured by VG Scientific.

The performance of the as-prepared electrodes was tested in three-electrode system. The reference electrode is saturated calomel electrode (SCE). The counter electrode is platinum electrode. The electrolyte is 3 M KOH solution. The working electrode was prepared by mixing the prepared CDs/NZP-10 composite with carbon black and polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) in a weight ratio of 8:1.5:0.5, followed by dropwise addition of an appropriate amount of N-methylpyrrolidone (NMP) solution. After stirring well for 8 h, the mixture was coated on a nickel foam (1*1 cm2) current collector. The prepared electrode was dried at 80 °C for 8 h in the vacuum. The mass loading of active material was approximately 2 mg cm−2. The negative electrode was prepared by mixing activated carbon with polytetrafluoroethylene in ethanol at a weight ratio of 9:1. The mixture containing the activated carbon was then coated on a nickel foam current collector and dried at 80 °C for 6 h in the vacuum.

The crystal structure and phase purity of the CDs/NZP-10 and CDs/NZP-0 composites were characterized by XRD measurements (Fig. 1 a). The diffraction peaks at 11.3°, 17.0°, 27.4°, 30.3°, 35.6°, and 36.8° are assigned to (020), (121), (230), (114), (214), and (161) planes of NiHPO4 phase (JCPDS No.39-0706), respectively. Meanwhile, the diffraction peaks at 11.3°, 20.6°, 21.9°, 23.5°, 27.1°, 30.1°, 35.0°, and 43.1° are referred to the corresponding (020), (122), (210), (023), (230), (114), (204) and (314) faces of ZnHPO4 phase (JCPDS No. 39-0707), respectively. Moreover, the excess nickel source tends to form monoclinic nickel phosphate. As shown in the XRD pattern, the (−112), (−221), (420), (−601), (131) and (004) crystal planes of Ni3(PO4)2 phase (JCPDS No. 38-1473) can be respectively detected at 37.2°, 45.2°, 53.2°, 56.5°, 62.1° and 63.9°. The XRD results proved that we have successfully synthesized nickel-zinc phosphate composite material.

To further explore the elemental composition and oxidation state of the samples, XPS measurements was applied. The doublets at 857.1 and 874.8 eV respectively corresponding to the binding energies (BEs) of Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2 represent the typical BEs of Ni2+. What's more, the peaks at 862.2 and 880.4 eV are typical shakeup satellites (Fig. 1b) [27]. The two peaks located at 1022.8 and 1046 eV are characteristic of Zn2+ (Fig. 1c). In the P 2p region (Fig. 1d), single BE peak at 133.4 eV is detected, which is assigned to the P-O bond. The BE of the O 1s detected at 531.4 eV could be corresponding to the P-O bond. In addition, other O 1s peaks of 532.9 eV and 533.4 eV result from adsorbed oxygen, hydroxide, and moisture (Fig. 1f) [28]. Among surface elements, carbon shows the highest atomic ratio, implying that CDs are mainly located on the surface of the material. As shown in Fig. 1e, the C 1s curve can be divided into three parts, including CC or CH bond (BE = 284.6 eV), CO or CN bond (BE = 285.9 eV) and OCO bond (BE = 288.8 eV) [29]. The above XPS data demonstrates that CDs are mainly located on the surface of CDs/NZP-10 composite with abundant organic functional groups. In addition, the C element shows the highest ratio compared to other elements on the surface of the composite, meaning that a large amount of CDs are coated around the nickel-zinc phosphate. This structure has two major benefits in terms of the promotion and protection. On the one hand, CDs can promote the requirement of high-rate applications in ion diffusion and charge transport kinetics [30]. On the other hand, CDs can inhibit phosphate dissolution and agglomeration [31], which is particularly important for long-term circulation. Therefore, this structure can improve the overall performance of the composite material. Furthermore, it also has a promotion in development new materials. Raman spectroscopy is a rapid and non-destructive method for characterizing the crystal structure of materials, the electronic energy band structure, phonon energy dispersion, and electron-phonon coupling [32,33]. Fig. 2 a shows Raman spectra of synthetic CDs/NZP-10 and CDs/NZP-0. The vibration mode is due to the PO4 tetrahedron (internal and external modes), and the octahedron coordinates the lattice pattern of transition metal atoms [34]. Among them, the O-P-O bending mode is located at 457 cm−1, symmetrical O-P-O stretching vibrations are observed at 965, 1023 and 1055 cm−1 [35], and asymmetric P-O-P stretching vibrations were obtained at 894 cm−1 [34]. All these models confirmed the formation of phosphate composites. As can be seen from black and red lines of Fig. 2b is a Raman plot of calcined carbon dots and the CDs/NZP-10 composite. From the picture, it can be seen that the D-band peak is around 1300 cm−1 and the G-band peak is around 1580 cm−1. D-band is used to explain the structural defects of carbon materials, G-band is used to illustrate the in-plane vibration of sp2 carbon atoms [36,37]. In addition, in the Raman spectrum of CDs/NZP-10, that the peak positions at about 968 cm−1 and 1024 cm−1 corresponding to Fig. 2a, indicating that it is NZP with the carbon.

To further characterize the morphology of the samples, FESEM, TEM and EDS characterizations were performed, as shown in Fig. S1 and Fig. 3 . For CDs/NZP-10 composites (Fig. S1c and d), mulberry-like morphology can be observed clearly. In the magnification picture (Fig. S1d), it can be seen that the surface of each large particle is rough and scattered with a diameter of approximately 200–300 nm. For comparison, samples with different concentrations of carbon dots and nickel or zinc phosphate composite were also tested. When the nickel-zinc phosphate composite without carbon dots, the morphology of the sample was irregular with some dispersibility (Fig. S1a and b). When the carbon dots concentration was increased to 0.5 mg mL−1 (Fig. S1e and f), the particles began to agglomerate and form a large block structure. With the carbon dots were increased to 2 mg mL−1, the morphology became looser and developed to more unstable (Fig. S1g and h). While the morphology of the nickel phosphate composite was a big sheet which stack to form a block structure. The zinc phosphate composite was only a block with bad dispersion (Fig. S3). The morphologies of CDs/NZP-10 nanostructure were further explored by TEM and HRTEM. It can be seen in Fig. 3b that each small nanoparticle is covered with smaller particle with the size of ∼25 nm. The precise passage between the particles facilitates the movement of electrolytic ions, while the rough surface of the particles can effectively promote the electronic storage and improve the rate performance. As shown in Fig. 3a, the HRTEM image of the CDs/NZP composite (CDs/NZP-10) shows that CDs and nickel-zinc phosphate particles uniformly combine to form a petal structure. The CDs were observed mainly at the edge of the composite, indicating that the CDs are mainly located on the surface of the composite and exist in the gap between the phosphate nanoparticles. The lattice planes with lattice spacings of 0.25 nm, 0.35 nm, and 0.21 nm are belong to the (011), (100) and (111) planes of carbon, respectively. The EDS measure was applied to analyze the stoichiometry of the CDs/NZP-10 element (Fig. 3c). The results revealed that the O, P, Zn, C and Ni elements were uniformly distributed in the composite. Fig. S2 showed the stoichiometric atomic ratio of Ni: Zn: P with 12.44: 4.21: 8.6. The C element was dispersed uniformly, which could be well consistent with the HRTEM results. However, the signal of CDs was amplified due to the carbon deposition.

When used as an electrode material, such structure is beneficial to allow the CDs contact with the electrolyte, and the functional groups on the CDs contribute to the tantalum capacitance and facilitate rapid transfer of charge carriers during charge and discharge [38,39].

In the traditional three-electrode system, we used cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) to perform the electrochemical performance of the nickel-zinc phosphate mulberry-like nanomaterials in 3.0 M KOH aqueous. Fig. 4 a shows the CV curves of the CDs/NP-10, CDs/NZP-0, CDs/NZP-5, CDs/NZP-10, and CDs/NZP-20 electrodes at the scan rate of 10 mV s−1. Comparing the CV curves of monometallic nickel phosphate, it can be seen that the specific capacitance get greatly enhanced when the introduced of zinc element, which may be attributed to the synergistic effect between the (NiHPO4)2·3H2O and (ZnHPO4)2·3H2O. The CDs/NZP-10 composite shows the largest integrated area, indicating a superior capacitive behavior. Thus, we further investigated the electrochemical performances of the CDs/NZP-10 composite in details. A pair of redox peaks are visible in each CV curves at various scan rates of 5–100 mV s−1 (Fig. 4b), indicating the super Faradic pseudo-capacitive behavior of the samples. When the scan rate reaches to 50 mV s−1, a clear peak can also be seen, revealing that the diffusion of ions was not limited. The inset of Fig. 4b is a linear relationship between the square root of the scan rate and the anodic and cathodic peak current densities, which can be assumed to be dominated by OH− diffusion. Fig. 4c shows the GCD curves of CDs/NZP-10 electrodes with different current densities (1, 2, 5, and 10 A g−1). The shape of the GCD curves means that the mulberry-like nickel-zinc phosphate electrode has a good electrochemical performance. However, the CV curves show a little asymmetrical, which means the low Coulomb efficiency and the irreversible electrochemical behaviors during charge and discharge. The slight potential drop respected the equivalent series resistance of the nickel-zinc phosphate [40]. In addition, there is a potential platform for each discharge curve. The typical pseudocapacitor behavior of transition metal compounds was caused by charge transfer reactions at the electrode/electrolyte interface or the electrochemical absorption/desorption processes [41,42].

Based on the GCD curves (Fig. 4c), the calculated specific capacitances are shown in Fig. 4d. The obtained specific capacitances for CDs/NZP-10 composite electrode are 1885.7, 1357.1, 921.4, and 590 F g−1 at the current densities of 1, 2, 5, and 10 A g−1, respectively. The high specific capacitance revealed the great potential for the high power application, which can be explained by the exchange mechanism. During the charge/discharge progress, sufficient time is necessary to guarantee the OH− transfer between the solution and the surface of the electrode material, which could be beneficial for the embedding/ejection. Compared with the higher current densities, the OH− has enough time for transmission at a low current density, which is beneficial for the charge storage, thus higher specific capacitance can be obtained.

Fig. 4e compares the cyclic stability of CDs/NZP-0, CDs/NZP-5 and CDs/NZP-10 at a current density of 5 A g−1. The capacity retention of nickel zinc phosphate without carbon loading (CDs/NZP-0) was only 74.3%. After the combination with CDs, the capacitance retention of CDs/NZP-5 and CDs/NZP-10 could reach to 96.7% and 98.7%, respectively, superior than that CDs/NZP-0. The remarkable electrochemical performance of CDs/NZP-10 composite can be attributed to the unique structures. Firstly, the large surface area and rough surface of the composite increased the interface area between the electrode and the electrolyte, providing more active sites for electrochemical reactions [43]. Secondly, the surface was constructed by nanoparticles, this structure could facilitate the ion diffusion and electron transport, increasing the rate performance of the composite. Thirdly, compared with the samples (CDs/NZP-0, CDs/NZP-5, and CDs/NZP-20) with irregular morphologies, mulberry-like nanoparticles about 20–30 nm provided a shorter path for carrier transport, benefiting for the superior electrochemical performance.

To evaluate the electrical conductivity of the prepared electrodes, EIS measurements (the frequency range of 100 kHz–0.01 kHz) of the CDs/NZP-10 and CDs/NZP-0 were carried out and shown in Fig. 4f. The impedance curves showed, a Z′ axis intercept, a semicircle at high frequency region and a slope line in the low frequency region, which is related to equivalent series resistance (ESR), Rct and Zw. CDs/NZP-5 displays larger semicircle diameter at high-frequency area and lower slop at low-frequency area, compared with initial CDs/NZP-0, demonstrating that too lower CDs contents could not improve the electrochemical impedance (Rct and Zw). Meanwhile, CDs content also occupy the active mass and contribute little capacitance, so CDs/NZP-5 presents a lower capacitance compared with CDs/NZP-0. Compared with the CDs/NZP-0 electrode, the CDs/NZP-10 sample shows a smaller radius in the high-frequency region, indicating the faster charge transfer rate. The steeper line along the imaginary axis in the low frequency region suggests electrolyte diffusion resistance. This result indicates that CDs have a positive effect on reducing the electrochemical impedance of electrode material. The introduction of more CDs (CDs/NZP-20) decreases the intrinsic resistance (X axis intercept), Rct and Zw values, but much more CDs will hinder the direct contact and could not contribute high capacitance [25]. In view of these, CDs/NZP-0 possesses higher capacitance than CDs/NZP-5 and CDs/NZP-20. When the content of CDs reaches a suitable value (CDs/NZP-10), the as-obtained CDs/NZP-10 exhibits the enhanced synergistic effect. In the results, the slope of the CDs/NZP-10 plot is significantly smaller than that of CDs/NZP-0, indicating that electrodes made from CDs/phosphate composites will perform better. Morphology is a critical factor for electrochemical performance, especially, two-dimensional nanomaterials show some unique features. It was observed by TEM that the edge of CDs/NZP-10 was sheet, and the electrons in the ultra-thin two-dimensional nanomaterial were not limited by the interaction between the layers. In addition, the large lateral size and ultra-thin thickness endow them ultra-high specific surface area. The thickness of a few atoms provides them the greatest mechanical flexibility [44]. In most cases, nickel-zinc phosphate composites show better performance than pure nickel phosphate composites, whereas zinc phosphate composite displays poor capacitance, as shown in Fig. S3 and S4. Thanks to the synergistic effects, the CDs/NZP-10 exhibit enhanced capacitive behavior.

In order to further determine the practical application of the CDs/NZP-10 electrode, an asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) was assembled, wherein the CDs/NZP-10 electrode serves as the positive electrode and activated carbon (AC) as the negative electrode, cellulose paper as the separation membrane, and 3 M KOH as the electrolyte, respectively. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the CV curve of assembled CDs/NZP-10//AC ASC device maintains a similar shape even at 100 mV s−1, indicating its rapid and reversible storage abilities as well as excellent pseudocapacitive behavior. It can be noted that the CV curves at all scan rates showed a combination of Faraday capacitance and electric double layer capacitance characteristics. When the scan rate was increased to 20 mV s−1, the shape of CV curve did not show significant deformation, indicating that the ASC device has a high electron transfer rate and excellent capacitance performance. Fig. 5b is a CV plot of the CDs/NZP-0//AC asymmetric capacitor, which has a smaller area compared to the CDs/NZP-10//AC, which means poorer capacitance performance. Fig. 5c shows the GCD curve of the CDs/NZP-10//AC ASC device at different current densities in a voltage window of 0–1.65 V. Compared with the GCD curve of the CDs/NZP-0//AC device (Fig. 5d), the CDs/NZP-10//AC shows a longer discharge time and better performance. Fig. 5f shows the Ragone plot of the energy density and power density of the ASC device to evaluate the overall electrochemical performance. The CDs/NZP-10//AC exhibits a high energy density of 33.7 Wh kg−1 (when the power density reaches 824.9 W kg−1), superior to CDs/NZP-0//AC (density of 28.6 Wh kg−1, power density 800 W kg−1). At the same time, when the power density of CDs/NZP-10//AC reaches to 8000 W kg−1, the energy density is still 12.4 Wh kg−1. The activate carbon has high stability after 2000 cycles at a current density of 5 A g−1 which is shown in Fig. S5. Fig. 5h shows the cycling performance of the CDs/NZP-10//AC at a current density of 2 A g−1. After 6000 charge and discharge tests, the asymmetric capacitor still has 90.6% retention compared with initial capacitance. Except form individual positive or negative electrodes, the ASC device still maintain good cyclic stability. The four parallel red LED bulbs are triggered and keep bright. The CDs/NZP-10//AC asymmetric supercapacitor shows a lowest performance at 1000 cycles, and then the performance gradually increased. For further study the effect of the nickel foam current collector whether provide capacitance after 1000 cycles, we tested the cycle performance after 6000 cycles was shown in Fig. S6. From the picture, we make sure that the nickel foam current collector could not provide capacitance after 1000 cycles. The interesting capacitance fade and increase phenomenon attributes to these reasons as follow: First, fast capacitance fade mainly attributes to structure damages in the initial cyclic measurement. Second, the capacity of nickel-zinc phosphate spheres with carbon dots can be improved due to the activation, which reconstructs the electrode structure. Active materials are greatly wetted by the electrolyte and the electrical/ionic conductivity is improved sufficiently. Third, more and more internal particles are exposed and activated in the repeated charge/discharge processes [45,46]. Table 1 lists the comparisons of related works in electrochemical performance, which shows the excellent comprehensive performance, low cost and easy preparation of CDs/NZP-10. The above data indicates that adding appropriate concentration of CDs increases comprehensive properties and CDs/NZP-10 has a good performance with more voltage.

In conclusion, we have successfully synthesized CDs and CDs/NZP composites via a simple hydrothermal method. The mulberry-like CDs/NZP-10 composites are composed of smaller phosphate nanoparticles, and CDs are mainly existed in the surface edge of phosphate nanoparticles, thus beneficial for fully contacting the electrolyte during the charge/discharge progress. Furthermore, the stability of carbon material, the high specific capacitance of functional groups existed in the CDs, as well as the synergistic effect between nickel and zinc, the CDs/NZP-10 electrode showed suitable electrochemical performance. As respected, the CDs/NZP-10 composite could reach a high capacitance of 1885.7 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 with wonderful stability (98.7% capacitance retention after 3000 cycles). And the assembled CDs/NZP-10//AC asymmetric device could achieve a suitable energy density of 33.7 Wh kg−1 (at 824.9 W kg−1), which owns the power to light up the LED indicator, indicating its voltage for practical application. Therefore, the comprehensive performance of phosphates could be significantly enhanced via introducing CDs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51501068).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2018.08.119.
